data.title: BARK signals accelerated revenue diversification and commerce growth to one-third of business amid tariff headwinds
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: BARK, Inc. (BARK) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Matt Meeker, CEO, emphasized three major takeaways: "First, we delivered our first ever adjusted EBITDA positive year. Second, we intend to remain adjusted EBITDA positive this year and beyond. And third, we plan to accelerate the diversification of our revenue faster than previously planned." Meeker detailed that Q4 adjusted EBITDA reached $5.2 million, marking the company's best quarterly result, and for the full year, BARK achieved $5.4 million in adjusted EBITDA. He explained the company pulled back on growth, citing "tariff-related uncertainty and potential downstream costs," and highlighted commerce business growth of 27% year-over-year to $68.3 million, with expanded retail partnerships. Gross margins hit a record 63.6% in Q4.
  • Meeker stated the company will "shift investment dollars more rapidly and aggressively in new product lines, distribution channels and services than previously planned," referencing the upcoming consumables line, new services from BARK Air, and acceleration into wholesale and e-commerce channels.
  • Zahir M. Ibrahim, CFO, said "Fiscal '25 was a significant year for BARK. We delivered our first full year of positive adjusted EBITDA, driven by ongoing margin expansion and improvements in operating efficiency."

Outlook

  • Ibrahim reported that, due to macro volatility and the evolving tariff landscape, the company is "unable to provide full year guidance at this time." For Q1, BARK expects total revenue between $99 million to $101 million, down 14% at the midpoint versus last year. Adjusted EBITDA for Q1 is projected between minus $1 million and $1 million.
  • Management highlighted that commerce is expected to constitute approximately one-third of the business over the next 2 to 3 years, up from 14% of total revenue in fiscal 2025.
  • Ibrahim explained, "By the end of fiscal 2026, we anticipate a more diversified and balanced sourcing footprint for our toy production."

Financial Results

  • BARK reported Q4 revenue of $115.4 million and full year revenue of $484.2 million, reflecting a 1.2% year-over-year decline. Commerce revenue reached $15.4 million in Q4 and $68.3 million for the year. BARK Air delivered $1.8 million in Q4 and nearly $6 million for the year. Consolidated gross margin rose to 63.6% in Q4. Total marketing expense in Q4 was $17.3 million.
  • The company ended the year with $94 million in cash, having spent $10.5 million to repurchase 6 million shares during the quarter and $18.5 million for 11.4 million shares in the full year. Inventory at year-end was $88 million.
  • G&A expenses were $114 million for the year, down $14 million. Shipping and fulfillment expenses were $139 million, nearly flat year-over-year.

Q&A

  • Maria Ripps, Canaccord, asked about supply chain diversification. Ibrahim responded, "We have the flexibility if we wanted to, to manufacture all of our toys outside of China by the end of this fiscal year."
  • Ripps inquired about the Shopify migration. Meeker said, "All of our active subscribers and new subscribers are coming through bark.co. All of our products are listed there...from a platform perspective, feeling really good about where we are with Shopify and how we're operating on it."
  • Ryan Meyers, Lake Street Capital, asked about D2C and commerce demand. Meeker explained the pullback in D2C marketing was due to tariffs and consumer sentiment, noting, "It's long overdue for us to really, really take this seriously and stop pouring every marketing dollar...into a toy business that has those dynamics and really isn't...the growth engine of the future."
  • Meyers also asked about commerce demand. Ibrahim said, "Commerce demand has been pretty strong. We grew 27% in fiscal '25...we expect Commerce to grow at a similar sort of level as what we saw in fiscal '25."
  • Kaumil Gajrawala, Jefferies, pressed on pricing and marketing. Meeker stated, "We're not fooling ourselves saying they were buying a $30 Barkbox, but now they'll pay $60...That's just not tenable."
  • Gajrawala asked about share buybacks. Ibrahim said, "We've been pretty aggressive about it through the quarter...we want that dry powder available to us as the year goes on."
  • Unidentified Analyst (Citigroup) asked about commerce segment pullbacks. Ibrahim said, "As we've started to see the tariffs come down...you're seeing that demand and the order placement coming back in."
  • The analyst also asked about the toy and consumables mix. Ibrahim clarified, "It's more heavily skewed today towards toys. I would say it's at least 90% is toys."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts asked probing questions about supply chain risk, revenue diversification, and marketing strategy, indicating a slightly cautious tone, especially regarding tariff exposure and D2C performance.
  • Management maintained a confident but measured tone, emphasizing adaptability and financial discipline. Phrases such as "we have a plan and are committed to achieving this" and "we know how to do it, and we have a plan" signaled confidence. During Q&A, management acknowledged challenges but reiterated a focus on maintaining EBITDA positivity and accelerating revenue diversification.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, there was a noticeable shift from optimism about revenue growth and D2C momentum to a more defensive posture, especially in response to tariff and consumer sentiment concerns.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The previous quarter featured a confident outlook on top-line growth, D2C stabilization, and commerce expansion, as well as a reaffirmation of revenue and EBITDA guidance. In Q4, management withdrew full-year guidance due to macro and tariff uncertainties.
  • The strategic focus has shifted from scaling D2C and building on Shopify momentum to accelerating diversification into new product lines and channels, as well as mitigating concentrated exposure to tariffs.
  • Commerce growth remained strong but was slightly tempered by tariff-related order timing. Management's tone moved from forward-looking optimism to a more cautious, resilient stance, while analysts' focus shifted toward risk mitigation and sustainability of profitability.
  • Key metrics such as revenue and gross margin saw modest changes, but the most notable development was the company's first full year of positive adjusted EBITDA.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited tariffs on Chinese imports—up to 145% at times—as a key risk, with efforts to diversify manufacturing and source outside China underway. Ibrahim pointed out, "Supplier transitions of magnitude carry risk and many key variables such as future tariff actions, trading policy, inflation and consumer response remain outside of our control."
  • There is macroeconomic uncertainty impacting consumer sentiment and D2C performance. Management is also contending with USPS rate changes.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the sustainability of D2C acquisition, the pace of commerce growth, and the company's ability to offset tariff impacts without sacrificing margins or customer retention.

Final Takeaway

BARK, Inc. marked its first full year of positive adjusted EBITDA while navigating significant tariff headwinds and softening consumer sentiment. Management aims to sustain profitability and is accelerating diversification away from D2C subscriptions to commerce, new products, and innovative services like BARK Air. The company is proactively adjusting its supply chain to mitigate tariff risk, investing in new growth channels, and maintaining disciplined cost control. While the near-term outlook is clouded by macro and policy uncertainty, BARK enters fiscal 2026 with a strengthened financial foundation and a clear strategy to drive revenue diversification and resilience.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Argan signals backlog to surpass $2B in 2025 amid power sector momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Argan, Inc. (AGX) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO David Hibbert Watson highlighted a strong start to fiscal 2026, noting "consolidated revenue growth of 23% to $193.7 million and gross margin of 19%, led by continued momentum in our Power Industry Services segment." Watson also reported "record backlog of $1.9 billion as of April 30, 2025," reflecting full notice to proceed on the Sandow Lakes Energy Company project for a 1.2-gigawatt natural gas-fired plant in Texas. He emphasized, "Power demand has reached its highest level in 2 decades and is expected to increase further with the development of AI data centers, the onshoring of complex manufacturing, and the growing adoption of electric vehicles."
  • Watson stated the company is "energized about the demand environment, which we believe will present attractive project opportunities for the next decade and beyond."
  • The balance sheet remains strong with $546.5 million of cash and investments, net liquidity of $315 million, and no debt. Watson also noted an increase in the size of the share repurchase program to $150 million.
  • Watson announced a management change: "Sean Terrell, who has served as President of TRC since 2023, was recently appointed to the additional role of Chief Executive Officer. This change came as Bobby Foister, Jr., stepped down to take a reduced role as part of a longstanding succession plan."
  • CFO Joshua S. Baugher stated, "First quarter revenues increased 23% to $193.7 million, primarily reflecting strong revenue growth in our Power Industry Services segment as compared to the first quarter of fiscal 2025."
  • Baugher continued, "For the 3-month period ended April 30, 2025, Argan reported consolidated gross profit of approximately $36.9 million, or gross margin of 19%."

Outlook

  • Watson projected continued backlog growth, stating, "In the short-to-medium term, we expect to add several power industrial jobs over the course of the next 6 months, which should put us significantly over $2 billion in backlog later this year."
  • He further shared, "In the long term, we believe demand will remain strong for the next decade and beyond, and this is underscored by the fact that the OEMs are starting to fill 2030 gas turbine slots as they are primarily sold out of earlier years."
  • For the Industrial Construction Services segment, Watson commented, "TRC's backlog increased to $91 million, and our confidence in this segment is really strong. Based on current visibility, we expect revenues to increase meaningfully over the next several quarters."

Financial Results

  • Baugher reported consolidated gross profit of $36.9 million, or gross margin of 19%, for the first quarter of fiscal 2026. Net income was $22.6 million, or $1.60 per diluted share. EBITDA for the quarter was $30.3 million, or 15.6% as a percent of revenue.
  • The Power Industry Services segment generated revenues of $160 million, representing 83% of first quarter revenues and pretax book income of approximately $31 million.
  • Industrial Construction Services contributed $29 million in revenue and pretax book income of approximately $2 million, with its segment gross margin at 10.8%.
  • Telecommunications Infrastructure Services contributed 2% of first quarter revenues, with a segment gross margin of 18%.
  • The company returned $18 million of capital to shareholders during the quarter, including a quarterly dividend of $0.375 and share repurchases or net settlements of approximately 100,000 shares for $12.9 million.

Q&A

  • Robert Duncan Brown, Lake Street Capital: "What's sort of the pipeline look like for the rest of the year? And could you give us some color on the size and potential there?" Watson responded, "The pipeline remains strong, and we're very bullish on being able to continue to add to the backlog... we expect to add several power industrial jobs over the course of the next 6 months, which should put us significantly over $2 billion in backlog later this year."
  • Brown: "What's sort of the potential that backlog can get to given your capacity and given sort of what you see in the project pipeline?" Watson replied, "That's the guidance, right? It's significantly over $2 billion. We have project capacity in that 10-plus range between renewable and gas jobs."
  • Brown: "On the Industrial business, I think this quarter was sort of bottoming. What's the outlook there?" Watson answered, "We expected a slight contraction in this past quarter, which there was, but we're still seeing strong interest in TRC... we expect revenues to increase meaningfully over the next several quarters."
  • Christopher Paul Moore, CJS Securities: "Gross margins... can you quantify or even estimate the amount of excess margin in there from projects like Trumbull?" Watson said, "They basically reflect continued strong execution across the business, as well as the continued changing mix of projects and contract types... we expect to exceed last year's margin profile as we move through the year."
  • Moore: "Is there an optimal backlog level if most of it is natural gas?" Watson explained, "Our backlog bounces around some given that overnight, we could add $600 million, $700 million, $500 million contract... the trajectory of backlog [is] to increase over the course of the year, though it could bounce around some."
  • Moore: "Is the 3- to 4-year project timeline permanent?" Watson replied, "It's primarily supply chain driven... it is currently a 3- to 4-year timeline typically, though smaller jobs could be shorter."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on backlog growth, margins, and industrial segment recovery, with a positive tone and congratulatory remarks, such as "Congratulations on a good quarter."
  • Management conveyed confidence and optimism, repeatedly highlighting robust demand, a strong pipeline, and financial discipline. Watson used phrases like "we're very bullish" and "we remain very optimistic about our continued growth."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management maintained a positive tone, with continued focus on backlog, margins, and multi-year growth visibility. Management's confidence appeared to increase, using more assertive language about growth potential and project pipeline.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Backlog increased from $1.4 billion at January 31, 2025, to $1.9 billion at April 30, 2025, with management now guiding for backlog to move "significantly over $2 billion" later in the year.
  • Revenue for Q1 2026 was $193.7 million, down sequentially from $232.5 million in Q4 2025, reflecting seasonality and project timing, while net income and EBITDA also decreased from prior quarter levels.
  • Gross margin for Q1 2026 was 19%, compared to 20.5% in Q4 2025, but management indicated expectations to "exceed last year's margin profile as we move through the year."
  • The mix of backlog shifted toward natural gas projects, increasing to 67% versus 54% in the prior quarter.
  • The Industrial Construction Services segment saw a drop in revenue quarter-over-quarter but is expected to rebound in coming quarters.
  • Management tone grew more confident, especially regarding long-term demand and backlog growth, while analysts maintained a positive and inquisitive stance, focused on execution and margin sustainability.

Risks and Concerns

  • Watson addressed the variable timing of project starts, noting, "we often don't control the start times and new projects, so it's tough for us to give an exact estimate."
  • Project timelines for new gas jobs have extended from 2.5–3 years to 3–4 years, primarily due to supply chain issues.
  • Management highlighted the need to maintain execution discipline and manage risk effectively as project mix and volume increase.
  • Analysts probed for margin sustainability and the extent to which recent outperformance is repeatable, reflecting a cautious stance on future profitability.

Final Takeaway

Argan, Inc. began fiscal 2026 with considerable momentum, achieving substantial revenue and backlog growth driven by power sector demand, particularly for natural gas-fired facilities. Management guided for backlog to surpass $2 billion in the near term, supported by a strong project pipeline and robust market drivers such as AI, manufacturing onshoring, and electrification trends. The company remains focused on disciplined project execution, prudent capital allocation, and leveraging its capabilities for continued growth in energy infrastructure, positioning itself as a key partner in the sector’s multi-year expansion.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Energy Transfer says U.S. to require licenses to export ethane to China
data.description:
Energy Transfer (NYSE:ET) disclosed Wednesday it received a letter from the U.S. Commerce Department requiring a license to export ethane to China, and that it will file for an emergency authorization to continue exporting.

Another ethane exporter, Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), said earlier on Wednesday it received notice from the department denying its emergency requests to export three proposed cargoes of ethane totaling 2.2M barrels to China.

Enterprise Products (EPD) had filed the emergency authorization requests after it received a letter requiring license authorization on May 23; Energy Transfer (NYSE:ET) said it obtained its letter on June 3.

The letters said exports of ethane pose an unacceptable risk of military end-use in China, according to filings from both companies.

The denial of Enterprise Products' (EPD) emergency requests calls into question whether it is just a short-term disruption, Vortexa's head of Americas analysis Samantha Hartke told Reuters.

"Near-term cargo reshuffles or resales could be necessary, as would a greater dependence on domestic storage," Hartke said, adding that short positions in ethane had mounted on Wednesday.

data.title: Tilly's outlines Q2 loss range of ($0.09) to $0.07 as company sees stabilization signs
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Tilly's (TLYS) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Hezy Shaked highlighted sequential improvement in comparable net sales, stating, "the comparable net sales trend of our business has continued to improve in fiscal May, starting the second quarter, with a decrease of just 2.2%. Consequently, we believe our merchandise assortment is on trend and moving us in the right direction, and we are encouraged to see signs of potential stabilization in our business."
  • Shaked addressed marketing and customer engagement efforts: "In early March, we launched our Tilly's TikTok shop... Our shop has grown to a level that began outperforming our daily order volume through Amazon in mid-April and continues to grow."
  • CFO Michael L. Henry reported, "Gross margin, including buying, distribution and occupancy expenses, was 19.8% of net sales compared to 21% of net sales last year. Product margins improved by 40 basis points compared to last year primarily due to higher initial markups, partially offset by increased inventory valuation reserves."
  • Henry discussed cost control, noting, "Total SG&A expenses were $44 million, which included noncash store asset impairment and other asset write-off charges of $1.2 million. The $1.1 million decrease in total SG&A compared to last year was primarily due to reduced store payroll and related benefits of $0.9 million and lower noncash asset write-off charges of $0.5 million, partially offset by increased marketing expenses of $0.7 million."

Outlook

  • Henry stated, "we estimate the following ranges for the second quarter of fiscal 2025: net sales of approximately $150 million to $158 million, translating to a comparable net sales range of a decrease of 5% to flat, respectively; SG&A of approximately $48 million to $49 million... earnings in the range of a net loss of approximately $2.7 million to net income of $2 million, respectively; and per share results of a net loss of $0.09 to net income of $0.07, respectively."
  • Management expects to end Q2 with 232 stores after closing 7 and opening 1 new store, compared to 247 at the end of Q2 last year. They also anticipate total liquidity at quarter-end between $106 million and $111 million, with cash and investments of $43 million to $48 million and undrawn borrowing capacity of $63 million.
  • Henry added, "we expect to remain a debt-free company throughout fiscal 2025. We estimate it would take a consistent comparable net sales decrease of approximately 10% or more over the course of the remainder of the fiscal year to require any level of borrowing this year."

Financial Results

  • Net sales for Q1 2025 were $107.6 million, a decrease of 7.1% year-over-year. Net sales from physical stores decreased by 7.4%, while e-commerce net sales decreased by 5.8%.
  • Comparable net sales decreased by 7%. Q1 ended with 238 stores, down 8 from last year.
  • Pretax loss was $22.3 million or 20.7% of net sales. Net loss was $22.2 million or ($0.74) per share compared to $19.6 million or ($0.65) per share last year.
  • Inventory and unit inventories were 3.8% and 10.9% lower, respectively, than the end of last year's first quarter. Total liquidity stood at $92.6 million.

Q&A

  • Matthew Butler Koranda, ROTH Capital, asked about quarterly cadence and transaction versus ticket breakdown. Henry responded, "through the first quarter, fiscal February was down 5.7%, March was down 13.8%, and then April was plus 1.5%. In terms of transactions, traffic was down low single digits... The average sale was down low single digits during the first quarter. It's actually up 1% so far in May... total transactions are down 5% to 6%."
  • Koranda queried about Q2 guidance and back-to-school trends. Henry said, "the bulk of the sales volume in the quarter is right at the end because we start the beginning of the back-to-school season in the back half of July. So that's what gives us some cautious optimism here with starting May at about a minus 2% and heading into what has been our strongest period of the year."
  • Koranda also questioned tariff impacts. Henry responded, "really not seeing a material impact over the remainder of the course of the year at this time. And obviously, the tariff discussion has been quite volatile. But at this stage, we'd expect our product margins to be consistent with LY, maybe a little better than LY at the better end of our range, maybe slightly worse than LY on the bottom end of our range."
  • Marni Shapiro, The Retail Tracker, asked about in-person events and merchandise improvements. Shaked replied, "it's the merchandise and the marketing that brings the people to stores, right? So we still have a lot of work to do, but it's more encouraging than we have seen in the last 1.5 years. I think if you look at the juniors side, it's becoming really spot on. The men's, we always did a decent job on that."
  • Richard Frederick Magnusen, B. Riley, inquired about activist investors and BDO outlook. Shaked said there have been no discussions with activists requesting Board seats. Henry commented, "with additional stores closing, some of the raw dollars of occupancy will come down. Whether we leverage or not will depend on our ability to get back to flat and then positive comps."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a slightly positive to neutral tone, focusing on improvements in sales trend, specifics on merchandise, and the impacts of tariffs and store events. There was measured optimism, especially regarding back-to-school season and product assortment.
  • Management's tone was cautiously optimistic in prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing sequential improvement and stabilization. Shaked stated, "there's no doubt that it's looking better and it's selling better. And the proof is that our traffic is up now we can say consistently in the last several weeks."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management appeared more confident, shifting from discussing organizational changes and cost reductions to highlighting positive sales momentum and improving customer engagement.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for second quarter net sales and per share results was more specific, with a range of ($0.09) to $0.07 per share, compared to last quarter's more cautious outlook.
  • Management's strategic focus shifted from heavy cost and inventory controls in Q4 to increased marketing and customer engagement activities in Q1, including TikTok Shop and influencer partnerships.
  • Analysts moved from pressing on merchandising changes and inventory to questions about sequential sales improvements and marketing initiatives.
  • Both management and analysts displayed greater confidence, with management expressing cautious optimism about stabilization and back-to-school season opportunities.

Risks and Concerns

  • The potential impact of tariffs on product costs remains a concern, though management currently views the impact as minor and mitigated through partner negotiations.
  • Store closures continue, with up to 15 additional closures possible depending on lease renewals.
  • Management noted ongoing volatility in consumer trends and macroeconomic factors but sees signs of stabilization through improved assortments and marketing efforts.

Final Takeaway

Tilly's management emphasized improving sales trends and customer engagement initiatives as contributing factors to early signs of business stabilization in Q1 2025. The company outlined a Q2 earnings range of ($0.09) to $0.07 per share and expects to remain debt-free throughout the year, while closely monitoring potential tariff impacts and continuing selective store closures. Management is increasingly optimistic about the effectiveness of new merchandising and marketing strategies, especially ahead of the pivotal back-to-school season.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Oil futures fall as Saudi Arabia said to seek more big OPEC output hikes
data.description:
Crude oil futures turned lower in a volatile session Wednesday, as a Bloomberg report said Saudi Arabia is open to additional significant OPEC+ production hikes after July in a bid for market share.

The Saudis want the group to add at least 411K bbl/day in August and potentially September, ideally as quickly as possible to take advantage of peak summer demand, according to the report.

Analysts said OPEC's intentions already have been well telegraphed, but the report would indicate that the cartel's current course likely will continue.

Oil futures got little support from a larger than expected 4.3M-barrel drawdown in U.S. crude oil stockpiles, the Energy Information Administration reported, which was offset by unexpectedly large increases in gasoline and distillate inventories of 5.2M barrels and 4.2M barrels, respectively.

"The big builds in gasoline and distillates were just head-scratchers, so much so that the market almost dismissed it out of hand," Phil Flynn of Price Futures Group said in commentary, adding that a drop in exports could have contributed to the big build in gasoline stocks, but the 1.2M bbl/day decline in gasoline demand is questionable and may be adjusted.

Shares of refiners fell broadly after the gasoline inventory build, including Valero Energy (VLO) -3.4%, Marathon Petroleum (MPC) -3.1%, Phillips 66 (PSX) -3%.

Meanwhile, Saudi Aramco lowered the official July selling price of its flagship Arab Light crude by $0.20/bbl for Asian buyers, after OPEC+ had continued with its production increases; the decrease was smaller than a $0.35 reduction anticipated in a survey of refiners and traders.

Also, production operations in Canada, some of which was shut-in due to wildfires, are beginning to restart.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for July delivery closed -0.9% to $62.85/bbl and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for August delivery ended -1.2% to $64.86/bbl, both down after two straight daily gains, while front-month Nymex July natural gas settled -0.1% to $3.716/MMBtu.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (CRAK)

data.title: ChargePoint outlines new Eaton partnership and anticipates margin improvement through innovation in fiscal 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ChargePoint Holdings, Inc. (CHPT) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Richard Wilmer, CEO, reported revenue of $98 million for the first quarter, stating it was "right within our guidance range." Wilmer emphasized, "Non-GAAP gross margin continues to increase quarter-over-quarter, reaching a new high of 31%. Notably, our gas subscription gross margin climbed to a record 60%, underscoring the strength of our SaaS-focused business model."
  • Wilmer highlighted the success of the DC fast charging program with General Motors, the extension of agreements with Mercedes-Benz, and strong market interest in the theft-resistant charging cable, which is set for production this summer. He noted be.ENERGISED now manages "over 700 charger models from over 85 different vendors," and outlined that ChargePoint has "over 352,000 ports under management, of which more than 35,000 are DC fast chargers, and more than 122,000 are located in Europe."
  • Wilmer announced a new partnership with Eaton, describing it as "the most exciting announcement of the year so far." He stated, "Together, ChargePoint and Eaton will deliver EV charging, electrical infrastructure, energy management and engineering services as the market's only end-to-end EV charging and power management solutions."
  • The CEO introduced a new AC hardware architecture, identifying it as "the first product line developed utilizing our lower-cost co-development structure" and anticipated it will enter the market at a competitive price, increasing margins and expanding U.S. and European market penetration.
  • Mansi Khetani, CFO, stated, "Revenue for the first quarter was $98 million, within our guidance range... Non-GAAP gross margin was 31%, improving by 1 percentage point sequentially and up 7 percentage points year-on-year."
  • Khetani noted, "We ended the quarter with $196 million in cash on hand. Q1 tends to be the quarter with highest cash usage due to the timing of some large annual payments."

Outlook

  • Khetani provided guidance for Q2 fiscal 2026: "we expect revenue to be $90 million to $100 million." She added, "We are guiding with caution due to the continued changes in the macro environment, including tariff uncertainty as well as our near-term focus on operationalizing our partnership with Eaton."
  • Management expects "revenue upside later in the year from the introduction of our new AC hardware... better performance in Europe and growth from our new partnership with Eaton."
  • The company reaffirmed its focus on "revenue growth, gross margin expansion and cost management to achieve our stated goal of being adjusted EBITDA positive in a quarter during fiscal 2026."

Financial Results

  • Khetani detailed, "Network charging systems at $52 million accounted for 53% of first quarter revenue. This was almost flat sequentially... Subscription revenue at $38 million was 39% of total revenue, essentially flat sequentially... Other revenue at $8 million was 8% of total revenue, down 31% sequentially."
  • First quarter billings percentages by vertical were: "commercial, 71%; fleet, 13%; residential, 12%; and other, 3%. From a geographic perspective, North America made up 85% of revenue and Europe was 15%."
  • Non-GAAP operating expenses were $57 million, up 9% sequentially and down 15% year-on-year. Non-GAAP adjusted EBITDA loss was $23 million, compared with a loss of $17 million in the prior quarter.
  • Inventory increased by $3 million to $212 million. "We anticipate that inventory balance will reduce gradually throughout the year, helping to free up cash."

Q&A

  • Colin William Rusch, Oppenheimer: Asked about the Eaton partnership and the pipeline for new systems growth. Wilmer responded that "there's a variety of forces at play, some positive, some causing caution, obviously, the macroeconomic conditions, tariffs and general uncertainty," but expressed excitement for the Eaton partnership, expecting it "to drive incremental growth" once fully operationalized by fiscal Q3.
  • Rusch followed up on international expansion outside Europe and North America. Wilmer stated, "At this point in time, we're focused on North America and Europe... the possibility definitely exists to penetrate new geographies as we move forward in the partnership."
  • Rusch inquired about inventory reduction cadence. Khetani explained, "we expect gradual reduction probably in the second quarter with a more meaningful reduction coming in the second half as we see revenue growth."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were neutral to slightly positive, focusing on pipeline opportunities, international expansion, and inventory reduction, with cautious optimism regarding the Eaton partnership's growth potential.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing innovation, resilience, and growth opportunities, but acknowledged macro uncertainty and near-term caution in the outlook. Khetani was pragmatic about cost controls and cash management.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts' tone shifted from questions about competitive dynamics and margin progress to more direct interest in new partnerships and revenue growth, with less skepticism about the company's market positioning. Management's confidence remained steady, bolstered by new strategic initiatives.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for Q2 fiscal 2026 is more cautious, with a revenue range lower at the midpoint than Q1's actual results. In the previous quarter, guidance for Q1 was $95 million to $105 million; this quarter, Q2 guidance is $90 million to $100 million.
  • The Eaton partnership and new AC hardware architecture represent new strategic shifts not present in Q4. Management continues to highlight margin improvement and innovation, but now with additional emphasis on integrated solutions and broader market access via Eaton.
  • Analysts' questions moved from competitive landscape and market share to granular details on partnership execution, international expansion, and working capital management.
  • Key metrics such as gross margin and adjusted EBITDA loss show improvement year-on-year but a sequential increase in operating expenses and adjusted EBITDA loss was noted for Q1.

Risks and Concerns

  • Wilmer stated, "Despite U.S. macroeconomic conditions and market uncertainty as well as the bottom line through the cost and operational actions we took last year," the business remains resilient.
  • The CEO noted "economic uncertainty" is impacting purchasing patterns, and macro conditions, tariffs, and policy uncertainty in the U.S. are headwinds.
  • Khetani reiterated guidance caution due to "continued changes in the macro environment, including tariff uncertainty as well as our near-term focus on operationalizing our partnership with Eaton."

Final Takeaway

ChargePoint's first quarter of fiscal 2026 featured solid revenue performance within guidance, margin expansion, and the launch of major strategic initiatives—a new partnership with Eaton and a lower-cost AC hardware platform—designed to accelerate growth, expand market access, and drive further margin improvements. Management remains focused on operational execution and cost control, guiding cautiously for Q2 amid macro uncertainty but emphasizing that incremental revenue from innovation and partnerships is expected to drive results as the year progresses.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Ecopetrol warns on security violations; CEO says received death threats
data.description:
Ecopetrol (NYSE:EC) CEO Ricardo Roa said Wednesday that the company has found 29 cases that could have violated security protocols and leaked confidential internal information, and Roa said he had received homophobic death threats.

The possible leaks were found during a digital analysis procedure within a cybersecurity protocol started in May, the company said, according to Reuters.

The CEO, who is openly gay, also said he has been targeted due to his sexual orientation.

Roa, who became Ecopetrol's (NYSE:EC) top executive in April 2023, faces a series of scandals and investigations that include a review by Colombia's National Electoral Council for the alleged violation of financing limits during President Petro's presidential campaign in 2022.

The company also said Colombia's Procurator General's Office visited its headquarters over a $5.8M legal contract with U.S. firm Covington & Burling, reportedly signed without board approval.

Ecopetrol (EC) has faced allegations of mismanagement and political interference by President Petro, who also reiterated his call for the company to divest its U.S. fracking operations.

data.title: Ruling prevents Apple from pausing court order on App Store purchases: report
data.description:
Apple's (NASDAQ:AAPL) request to pause a court order, which allowed App Store developers to avoid paying commission for in-app purchases, was denied today in a federal appeals court in California, according to Bloomberg.

The ruling stems from a half-decade long legal battle with Epic Games, the creator of Fortnite. Apple hoped to block the order as it appeals an April ruling that determined the company was violating California's anticompetitive laws by taking a commission on in-app purchases, the report said.

Epic Games, backed by China's Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY)(OTCPK:TCTZF), has been in a legal battle with Apple since 2020 over the iPhone maker charging commission fees on in-app payments.

The dispute started after Apple removed the Fortnite game from the App Store because Epic had developed a workaround to keep from paying the 30% fee on customers' in-app purchases.

Fortnite, the popular multiplayer shooter game, was banned in the app stores of Apple and Google (GOOG)(GOOGL) back in 2020 for violating in-app payment rules. The game returned to Google's Android devices worldwide and iPhones in the EU last year. It returned to Apple's App Store last month.

data.title: Baltic dry bulk index jumps to two-month high as larger vessel rates firm
data.description:
The Baltic Exchange dry bulk sea freight index extended gains to reach a two-month peak Wednesday, helped by an increase in rates for larger vessels, Hellenic Shipping News reported.

The main index, which monitors rates for capesize, panamax and supramax shipping vessels, jumped more than 4% to 1,489, its highest since April 4 and up for the sixth straight session.

The capesize index surged more than 6% to 2,481, its highest since March 27, as average daily earnings for capesize vessels, which typically transport 150K-ton cargoes such as iron ore and coal, added $1,226 to $20,576/day.

The panamax index rose more than 3% to 1,145, a one-week high, as average daily earnings for panamax vessels, which usually carry 60K-70K tons of coal or grain, rose $334 to $10,307/day.

Among smaller vessels, the supramax index ticked 2 points lower to 940, its lowest since April 15.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:BDRY), (NYSEARCA:SEA), (NYSEARCA:BOAT)

data.title: Shift4 Payments announces Isaacman’s resignation as CEO
data.description:
Shift4 Payments’ (NYSE:FOUR) Jared Isaacman has resigned from his role as the Chief Executive Officer, the payments processor said on Wednesday.

The company named Taylor Lauber as Isaacman’s successor and principal executive officer, effective June 5, 2025. Isaacman has been appointed as the Executive Chairman of the company.

Lauber’s appointment is part of the company’s previously announced succession plan. He has also been appointed to serve as a Class I member of the board, with a term to expire at the 2027 annual meeting of stockholders.

Earlier in the week, it was said that Isaacman is expected to continue as Shift4 Payments' CEO after President Donald Trump withdrew his nomination of the executive as the NASA administrator over the weekend. Last year, Trump nominated Isaacman, who's also an astronaut, to lead the NASA.

The company’s shares fell marginally in extended trading on Wednesday.
